title: AMD Pivots From HBM to LPDDR5X For New Versal Premium Gen 2 Memory on Package Chips
gist: AMD's ex-Xilinx FPGA division is abandoning HBM for LPDDR5X on-package memory in its Versal Premium Gen 2 line, because the AI boom made HBM supply scarce and short-lived, forcing discontinuation of the HBM-equipped Versal family barely two years after production began.

insights.claim: AMD is abandoning HBM in favor of LPDDR5X on-package memory for its Versal Premium Gen 2 adaptive SoCs/FPGAs, marking a hard pivot in its programmable-logic on-package memory strategy.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Signals that HBM scarcity is now forcing product-architecture changes at second-tier HBM consumers, benefiting LPDDR5X suppliers and freeing HBM for AI accelerators.
insights.quote: In short: HBM is out, and LPDDR5X is in. And that alone comes with quite the story.

insights.claim: On-package LPDDR5X gives AMD a more integrated, turn-key FPGA product aimed at customers wanting faster product development, addressing the limitations of off-package memory on standard Versal Premium Gen 2 parts.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
entities: Versal Premium Gen 2 Memory on Package
insights.so_what: Product differentiation shifts from HBM bandwidth toward integration/time-to-market, changing the FPGA value proposition.

insights.claim: AMD began discontinuing its Versal HBM adaptive SoC family in September 2025 due to a lack of HBM2e supply, barely two years after the chips went into production.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that non-AI HBM buyers are being squeezed out of allocation entirely, a leading indicator of severity in the memory crunch.
insights.quote: AMD began the process to discontinue the chip family in September of 2025 for a lack of HBM2e supply.
insights.locator: Barely two years after the Versal HBM series

insights.claim: The big three memory makers were winding down HBM2e production to shift lines to HBM3, eliminating AMD's ability to produce more Versal HBM chips.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: HBM fabs prioritize the newest, highest-margin generation for AI, stranding older-node HBM customers; watch for similar squeezes on other HBM2/2e users.
insights.quote: The big three memory manufacturers were already in the process of winding down their HBM2e production in order to shift those production lines over to HBM3

insights.claim: The AI boom drove a massive demand shift into HBM-heavy processors, and while AMD benefited via Instinct accelerators and EPYC CPUs, its programmable-logic division lost out as needed HBM became scarce then unavailable.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Same company can be both winner and loser of the AI/HBM boom depending on division; internal HBM allocation flows to the highest-value AI products first.
insights.quote: AMD's programmable logic division was a loser in this shift as the HBM they needed became scarce before finally becoming unavailable altogether.
insights.locator: The explosion in demand for computing hardware for AI

insights.claim: HBM went through three major specifications in its first nine years (excluding 'E' variants), and designers/manufacturers phase out older HBM far faster than DDR, which has a much longer demand tail.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Any product relying on HBM inherits a short supportable lifespan; long-life industrial/embedded designs should avoid HBM dependency.
insights.quote: HBM went through three major specifications in just the first nine years of its existence

insights.claim: AMD/Xilinx FPGAs are typically offered for extreme longevity, with standard UltraScale+ FPGAs committed to be available until 2045, creating a mismatch when paired with short-lived HBM.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The 2045 support horizon vs HBM's fast obsolescence explains why on-package HBM was structurally risky and why LPDDR5X is a safer long-term bet.
insights.quote: earlier this year AMD announced that they're going to be offering the normal UltraScale+ FPGAs until 2045

insights.claim: Bringing memory on-package makes chip production dependent on both logic fabs (TSMC) and memory suppliers, and HBM's limited-supplier history (only SK hynix made HBM1; Samsung and Micron joined at HBM2) compounded the risk.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: On-package memory adds a second supply-chain dependency; thin HBM supplier base amplifies single-point-of-failure risk for integrated products.
insights.quote: bringing memory on-package means that chip production is now reliant on both logic fabs (i.e. TSMC) and memory providers to supply the necessary HBM

insights.claim: Xilinx pioneered on-package memory FPGAs with the 2018 Virtex UltraScale+ HBM line (up to 16GB HBM1), followed by Versal HBM adaptive SoCs with up to 32GB of HBM2e.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes the installed base of HBM-FPGA customers now facing forced migration to LPDDR5X or requalification.
insights.quote: their Virtex UltraScale+ HBM FPGAs, which were launched in 2018 and offered up to 16GB of the high-density, high-performance HBM (1) memory
insights.locator: Even before being acquired by AMD

insights.claim: AMD framed HBM-equipped FPGAs as a deliberate gamble, betting the short HBM lifespan would still last long enough to meet customer needs, a bet it was winning until the AI boom.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Illustrates how AI-driven demand can invalidate an otherwise-sound long-tail supply strategy almost overnight.
insights.quote: For several years it was a bet that AMD was winning. Right up until the AI boom took off.
insights.locator: As a result of the smaller and faster market for HBM

insights.claim: The Versal Premium Gen 2 Memory on Package announcement lands on the final day to place orders for Versal HBM chips, tying end-of-life of the old line to the launch of the new.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
entities: Versal Premium Gen 2 Memory on Package
insights.so_what: Hard last-order deadline forces existing HBM-FPGA customers to decide now, accelerating LPDDR5X migration timelines.
insights.quote: which incidentally falls on the final day to place orders for Versal HBM chips
